text: Temperature projections from CMIP6 models indicate a 2.4°C warming by 2100 under the intermediate emissions scenario.
The CMIP6 ensemble shows substantial spread in precipitation projections over the Mediterranean basin.
We evaluate the performance of 32 CMIP6 models against observational records from 1950 to 2014.
Arctic sea ice extent simulated by CMIP6 models declines more rapidly than in previous generations.
The CMIP6 multi-model mean projects a 0.5 m rise in global mean sea level by 2100 under SSP5-8.5.
Systematic biases in CMIP6 tropical precipitation patterns remain a persistent challenge for regional climate attribution.
We bias-correct CMIP6 outputs using quantile delta mapping before downscaling to a 5 km grid.
The equilibrium climate sensitivity estimated from CMIP6 models ranges from 1.8 to 5.6°C.
CMIP6 projections are used to assess future flood risk in the Mekong River basin through 2080.
Compared to CMIP5, CMIP6 models show improved representation of the Southern Ocean heat uptake.
CMIP5 simulations were used to evaluate the role of internal variability in observed warming trends.
The CMIP5 archive provides a comprehensive dataset for assessing model spread in carbon cycle feedbacks.
We compare historical precipitation trends across 25 CMIP5 models over the South Asian monsoon region.
CMIP5 projections suggest an intensification of the global hydrological cycle under RCP 8.5 forcing.
The CMIP5 ensemble underestimates observed warming in the Arctic by approximately 0.3°C per decade.
Surface wind speed trends simulated by CMIP5 models show large inter-model variability over Europe.
CMIP5 multi-model projections indicate a northward shift of the jet stream under greenhouse gas forcing.
We assess drought frequency changes using the CMIP5 ensemble under two representative concentration pathways.
The thermal expansion component of sea level rise is quantified using CMIP5 ocean temperature outputs.
CMIP5 models consistently project a weakening of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation by 2100.
Under RCP 8.5, global mean surface temperature is projected to increase by 3.7°C relative to the pre-industrial baseline.
The RCP 8.5 scenario assumes continued heavy reliance on fossil fuels throughout the 21st century.
Extreme precipitation events become significantly more frequent under RCP 8.5 across tropical and subtropical regions.
We compare wildfire risk projections under RCP 8.5 and RCP 4.5 to assess mitigation benefits.
Ocean acidification levels under RCP 8.5 threaten coral reef ecosystems across the Indo-Pacific by mid-century.
The RCP 8.5 pathway leads to Arctic summer sea-ice-free conditions before 2050 in most model simulations.
Crop yield reductions of up to 25% are projected under RCP 8.5 without adaptation in South Asia.
Permafrost thaw rates under RCP 8.5 are projected to accelerate carbon release from boreal soils.
The difference in sea level rise between RCP 8.5 and RCP 2.6 reaches 0.4 m by 2100.
Heat stress indices under RCP 8.5 exceed human physiological limits in parts of the Persian Gulf.
Under RCP 4.5, global warming is projected to stabilize near 2.4°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100.
RCP 4.5 assumes moderate mitigation policies and a peak in greenhouse gas emissions around 2040.
We assess changes in streamflow variability under RCP 4.5 using a hydrological model forced by bias-corrected GCM output.
The frequency of category 4 and 5 hurricanes increases significantly even under RCP 4.5 forcing.
Ecosystem transitions in the boreal zone under RCP 4.5 are projected to affect 30% of current forest area.
Snow water equivalent declines by 40% in the Alps under RCP 4.5 by the end of the century.
RCP 4.5 projections suggest a reduction in annual runoff across the Mediterranean region of up to 20%.
The RCP 4.5 scenario results in significantly lower flood risk compared to RCP 8.5 in European river basins.
Urban heat island effects are amplified under RCP 4.5 due to combined greenhouse gas and land use changes.
Monsoon precipitation variability under RCP 4.5 shows a complex regional pattern across Southeast Asia.
Under RCP 2.6, warming is limited to approximately 1.6°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100.
RCP 2.6 requires rapid and deep decarbonization of the global energy system within the next two decades.
Arctic sea ice extent recovers partially under RCP 2.6 after mid-century in ensemble projections.
The RCP 2.6 pathway assumes large-scale deployment of negative emissions technologies to offset residual emissions.
Coral bleaching events remain frequent under RCP 2.6 despite the lower warming trajectory.
Projected changes in global crop production under RCP 2.6 are substantially smaller than under higher forcing scenarios.
The difference in permafrost extent between RCP 2.6 and RCP 8.5 reaches 6 million km² by 2100.
Stratospheric ozone recovery is projected to occur earlier under RCP 2.6 due to lower methane concentrations.
Sea level rise commitment under RCP 2.6 is substantially reduced compared to higher emission scenarios.
RCP 2.6 projections show a stabilization of global mean sea level rise below 0.5 m by 2100.
Under SSP2-4.5, global mean temperature is projected to reach approximately 2.7°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100.
SSP2-4.5 represents a middle-of-the-road scenario with moderate socioeconomic challenges and climate mitigation efforts.
We assess future water stress under SSP2-4.5 across 180 river basins using an ensemble of CMIP6 models.
Flood frequency under SSP2-4.5 increases significantly in tropical regions while decreasing in parts of Central Asia.
The SSP2-4.5 scenario leads to substantial biodiversity loss in tropical forest ecosystems by mid-century.
Projected changes in the frequency of atmospheric rivers under SSP2-4.5 have major implications for West Coast precipitation.
Heat-related mortality risk doubles under SSP2-4.5 compared to current climate conditions in Mediterranean cities.
SSP2-4.5 projections indicate that the Sahel will experience increased rainfall variability despite a drying trend.
The AMOC weakening under SSP2-4.5 is projected to be 20–30% by 2100 relative to present day.
SSP2-4.5 forcing results in a 15% reduction in global glacier volume by 2100 in most CMIP6 models.
Under SSP5-8.5, global mean temperatures could exceed 4.5°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100 in high-sensitivity models.
SSP5-8.5 is considered a high-end emission scenario driven by fossil fuel-intensive economic growth.
Extreme heat events under SSP5-8.5 are projected to affect over 3 billion people annually by 2080.
The SSP5-8.5 scenario leads to near-complete loss of summer Arctic sea ice by 2050 in all CMIP6 models.
Wildfire burned area under SSP5-8.5 could double in the Western United States by the end of the century.
SSP5-8.5 projections suggest that global food insecurity could affect an additional 500 million people by 2080.
The SSP5-8.5 pathway results in widespread permafrost degradation across Siberia and northern Canada.
Storm surge heights under SSP5-8.5 are projected to exceed 1.5 m in many low-lying coastal cities.
Tropical cyclone intensification under SSP5-8.5 poses significant risks to small island developing states.
SSP5-8.5 leads to the crossing of multiple tipping points in the Earth climate system before 2100.
Under SSP1-2.6, warming is projected to remain below 1.8°C in the majority of CMIP6 model simulations.
SSP1-2.6 assumes rapid societal transformation toward sustainability, including dietary shifts and reduced land use emissions.
The difference in projected sea level rise between SSP1-2.6 and SSP5-8.5 reaches nearly 0.6 m by 2150.
Under SSP1-2.6, the probability of exceeding the 1.5°C Paris Agreement threshold remains below 50%.
Ecosystem restoration goals are achievable under SSP1-2.6 but require immediate and sustained policy action.
SSP1-2.6 projections show a stabilization of global mean precipitation with minimal changes in monsoon intensity.
The SSP1-2.6 scenario results in the lowest projected frequency of extreme precipitation events among all SSPs.
Species extinction risk under SSP1-2.6 is substantially lower than under higher emission pathways.
SSP1-2.6 maintains Arctic summer sea ice cover in at least some years throughout the 21st century.
Carbon budget estimates under SSP1-2.6 require global net-zero emissions to be achieved by approximately 2050.
Under SSP3-7.0, regional conflicts over water resources are projected to intensify due to increased drought frequency.
SSP3-7.0 is characterized by a fragmented world with high emissions and limited international cooperation on climate.
Projected warming under SSP3-7.0 reaches approximately 3.6°C by 2100 in the CMIP6 multi-model mean.
SSP3-7.0 results in severe reductions in crop yields across sub-Saharan Africa due to combined heat and drought stress.
The SSP3-7.0 scenario leads to substantial increases in vector-borne disease risk across tropical regions.
Under SSP3-7.0, the Amazon rainforest faces increased risk of dieback due to reduced precipitation and warming.
SSP3-7.0 projections indicate a substantial increase in the frequency of compound flood events in coastal Asia.
The SSP3-7.0 pathway results in a loss of over 40% of current glacier volume in the Hindu Kush by 2100.
Atmospheric CO2 concentrations under SSP3-7.0 reach approximately 700 ppm by 2100.
SSP3-7.0 represents a challenging mitigation scenario requiring large-scale carbon capture to limit further warming.
HadGEM3 simulates a stronger Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation response to greenhouse gas forcing than most CMIP6 models.
We evaluate the performance of HadGEM3 in reproducing observed Northern Hemisphere blocking frequency.
HadGEM3 projects a significant poleward expansion of the Hadley cell under high emission scenarios.
The cloud feedback parameterization in HadGEM3 contributes to its relatively high equilibrium climate sensitivity.
HadGEM3 shows improved representation of tropical precipitation variability compared to its predecessor HadGEM2.
We use HadGEM3 output to assess future changes in extratropical cyclone frequency over the North Atlantic.
HadGEM3 simulates an earlier onset of Arctic summer sea-ice-free conditions compared to the CMIP6 ensemble median.
The land surface scheme in HadGEM3 has been updated to better represent permafrost dynamics.
HadGEM3 projections indicate a substantial increase in UK summer rainfall extremes under SSP5-8.5.
Temperature biases in HadGEM3 over the Southern Ocean are smaller than in previous UK Met Office models.
CESM2 exhibits one of the highest equilibrium climate sensitivities among CMIP6 models at approximately 5.2°C.
We use CESM2 large ensemble simulations to partition internal variability from forced climate change signals.
CESM2 projects significant drying trends over the American Southwest under SSP5-8.5 by mid-century.
The ocean biogeochemistry module in CESM2 enables coupled carbon cycle projections through 2100.
CESM2 simulates the observed increase in atmospheric CO2 concentration over the industrial period with high accuracy.
We compare CESM2 and CanESM5 projections of Arctic sea ice thickness under SSP2-4.5.
CESM2 historical simulations show good agreement with observed global mean surface temperature trends since 1950.
The land ice module in CESM2 allows for interactive ice sheet dynamics in long-term climate simulations.
CESM2 projects a 30% reduction in Colorado River annual streamflow under SSP5-8.5 by 2080.
Aerosol forcing in CESM2 has been updated to improve representation of observed 20th century cooling periods.
MPI-ESM1-2 projects moderate warming of 2.8°C under SSP3-7.0, lower than the CMIP6 multi-model mean.
We evaluate the MPI-ESM1-2 representation of European blocking events against reanalysis data.
MPI-ESM1-2 simulates a realistic interannual variability of the North Atlantic Oscillation.
The carbon cycle in MPI-ESM1-2 shows a strong terrestrial carbon sink in high-latitude ecosystems.
MPI-ESM1-2 projects an intensification of the West African monsoon under SSP5-8.5.
We use MPI-ESM1-2 output to assess changes in Mediterranean cyclone frequency under future warming scenarios.
MPI-ESM1-2 historical simulations accurately reproduce observed sea surface temperature trends in the tropical Pacific.
The ocean heat uptake in MPI-ESM1-2 is consistent with Argo float observations over the past two decades.
MPI-ESM1-2 projects a significant reduction in Central European summer precipitation by the end of the century.
Stratospheric ozone representation in MPI-ESM1-2 improves the simulation of polar vortex dynamics.
IPSL-CM6A exhibits a high climate sensitivity and projects substantial warming under all SSP scenarios.
We evaluate IPSL-CM6A performance in simulating the observed variability of the El Niño-Southern Oscillation.
IPSL-CM6A projects a significant intensification of tropical cyclones in the Bay of Bengal under SSP5-8.5.
The land surface model in IPSL-CM6A includes dynamic vegetation that responds to CO2 fertilization effects.
IPSL-CM6A simulates a stronger-than-observed positive feedback between soil moisture and precipitation in the Sahel.
We compare IPSL-CM6A and MIROC6 projections of Saharan greening under high CO2 scenarios.
IPSL-CM6A historical simulations show a warm bias over the North African continent in boreal summer.
The atmospheric general circulation in IPSL-CM6A is evaluated using ERA5 reanalysis as a reference dataset.
IPSL-CM6A projects a significant poleward displacement of the Southern Hemisphere storm track under SSP2-4.5.
Permafrost coverage in IPSL-CM6A decreases by 40% under SSP5-8.5 due to strong Arctic amplification.
ERA5 reanalysis data are used to evaluate the performance of dynamical downscaling models over complex terrain.
We bias-correct ERA5 temperature and precipitation fields before using them to drive a hydrological model.
ERA5 provides hourly estimates of atmospheric variables at a horizontal resolution of approximately 31 km.
Observed warming trends derived from ERA5 are consistent with surface station records over the global land area.
We use ERA5 wind speed data to assess changes in near-surface wind energy resources over Europe.
ERA5 reanalysis is used to validate the simulation of extreme precipitation events in regional climate models.
The ERA5 dataset covers the period from 1940 to present, providing a long-term record for climate trend analysis.
We compare ERA5 and ERA-Interim reanalysis products to assess improvements in the representation of Arctic clouds.
ERA5 soil moisture data are used to initialize land surface conditions in seasonal climate forecasts.
Atmospheric river frequency and intensity derived from ERA5 are used to assess climate change impacts on California precipitation.
ERA-Interim was replaced by ERA5 as the primary reanalysis product of the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts.
We use ERA-Interim to assess long-term trends in Northern Hemisphere jet stream variability.
ERA-Interim reanalysis data are used to evaluate the ability of regional climate models to simulate observed precipitation.
The representation of the quasi-biennial oscillation in ERA-Interim has been extensively validated against radiosonde data.
We compare ERA-Interim and NCEP reanalysis products to assess uncertainty in tropical cyclone track climatology.
ERA-Interim provides a dynamically consistent dataset for studying observed changes in global atmospheric circulation.
Surface energy balance components from ERA-Interim are used to validate land surface model simulations.
ERA-Interim precipitation fields are evaluated against satellite-based estimates over data-sparse ocean regions.
We use ERA-Interim wind data to assess changes in the frequency of blocking events over the North Atlantic.
The 6-hourly ERA-Interim fields are used to characterize synoptic-scale variability in the Southern Hemisphere.
NCEP reanalysis data are used to assess the skill of seasonal climate forecasts over the North American continent.
We compare NCEP and ERA5 reanalysis products to evaluate uncertainty in Arctic surface temperature trends.
NCEP reanalysis is used to validate the simulation of El Niño teleconnections in coupled climate models.
The NCEP reanalysis provides a long-term record of atmospheric circulation extending back to 1948.
We use NCEP reanalysis data to characterize the large-scale atmospheric drivers of East African drought.
NCEP reanalysis products are used to evaluate the representation of the Madden-Julian Oscillation in climate models.
Surface pressure fields from NCEP are used to identify and track extratropical cyclone activity over the North Pacific.
We assess trends in stratospheric polar vortex strength using the NCEP reanalysis dataset from 1950 to present.
NCEP reanalysis data are used to initialize the atmospheric component of seasonal prediction systems.
The NCEP reanalysis has known biases in the Southern Ocean that should be considered when evaluating model performance.
CFSR provides a high-resolution global reanalysis of atmospheric, oceanic, and land surface conditions.
We use CFSR to evaluate the ability of regional climate models to simulate observed precipitation over Central America.
CFSR reanalysis data are used to assess the climatological characteristics of atmospheric blocking over the North Pacific.
The CFSR dataset is used to validate the representation of the South Asian monsoon onset in coupled models.
We compare CFSR and ERA5 products to assess uncertainty in tropical cyclone intensity climatology.
CFSR provides six-hourly data at approximately 38 km horizontal resolution for the period 1979 to present.
Surface wind stress fields from CFSR are used to drive an ocean general circulation model in hindcast mode.
We use CFSR soil temperature data to evaluate the performance of land surface models in cold regions.
CFSR reanalysis is employed to characterize the large-scale atmospheric circulation associated with European heat waves.
Sea ice concentration data from CFSR are compared with satellite observations to assess reanalysis quality.
GCM projections suggest an increase in the frequency of extreme precipitation events over most tropical land areas.
We compare the climate sensitivity of multiple GCMs to identify sources of uncertainty in future warming estimates.
A GCM ensemble of 30 models is used to assess the robustness of projected changes in the monsoon circulation.
GCM output is statistically downscaled to a 1 km resolution for hydrological impact assessments.
The GCM used in this study has a horizontal atmospheric resolution of approximately 1.25° latitude by 1.875° longitude.
GCM simulations are evaluated against observational datasets to assess systematic biases in the historical period.
We use a perturbed parameter ensemble of a single GCM to explore the role of parameterization uncertainty.
GCM projections of Arctic amplification are consistent with observed warming patterns over the past four decades.
The atmospheric component of the GCM is coupled to a dynamic vegetation model to allow land-atmosphere feedbacks.
GCM projections show large inter-model spread in precipitation changes over the Amazon basin under SSP5-8.5.
RCM simulations driven by GCM boundary conditions show added value over complex terrain regions such as the Alps.
We assess the ability of an RCM to capture observed mesoscale precipitation patterns over the Iberian Peninsula.
The RCM is run at 12 km resolution to resolve sub-GCM-scale processes relevant for regional impact assessments.
RCM projections of summer precipitation over Central Europe show a strong drying signal by the end of the century.
We evaluate the RCM simulation of observed temperature extremes using a suite of climate indices.
RCM ensembles driven by multiple GCMs are used to quantify the contribution of model uncertainty to regional projections.
The RCM boundary conditions are taken from a CMIP6 GCM simulation under the SSP3-7.0 scenario.
RCM simulations show significant improvements in the representation of convective precipitation compared to coarser GCMs.
We use RCM output to assess changes in wind energy potential over northern Europe under SSP2-4.5.
The RCM captures the observed orographic enhancement of precipitation in the Scandinavian Mountains with high fidelity.
IPCC AR6 projects a likely warming range of 1.5–4.5°C for a doubling of atmospheric CO2.
The IPCC AR6 synthesis report concludes that human influence has warmed the climate at an unprecedented rate.
We use the climate sensitivity ranges assessed in IPCC AR6 to constrain projections of 21st century warming.
IPCC AR6 identifies tipping elements in the climate system that could be activated at warming levels above 1.5°C.
The IPCC AR6 Working Group I report provides a comprehensive assessment of observed and projected climate changes.
Policy-relevant scenarios in IPCC AR6 are based on the Shared Socioeconomic Pathway framework.
IPCC AR6 concludes that limiting warming to 1.5°C requires reaching net-zero CO2 emissions by around 2050.
The regional climate projections in IPCC AR6 are presented through the Interactive Atlas tool.
IPCC AR6 assesses the attribution of observed changes in extreme weather events to human-caused climate change.
The carbon budget estimates in IPCC AR6 are consistent with those derived from the CMIP6 model ensemble.
The IPCC AR5 report projected a global mean sea level rise of 0.26–0.82 m by 2100 under various scenarios.
We compare IPCC AR5 and IPCC AR6 projections of Arctic sea ice loss to assess changes in scientific understanding.
IPCC AR5 concluded that warming of the climate system is unequivocal, with evidence from multiple observational records.
The IPCC AR5 Working Group II report assessed the impacts of climate change on natural and human systems.
Climate sensitivity estimates in IPCC AR5 ranged from 1.5 to 4.5°C, consistent with expert judgment since the 1970s.
IPCC AR5 identified ocean acidification as a major threat to marine biodiversity under all emission scenarios.
The mitigation scenarios assessed in IPCC AR5 showed that limiting warming to 2°C requires substantial emissions reductions.
IPCC AR5 highlighted the importance of adaptation measures in regions already experiencing significant climate impacts.
The IPCC AR5 cryosphere chapter documented accelerating mass loss from the Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets.
Regional temperature projections in IPCC AR5 showed robust warming signals over all land areas by 2100.
ECMWF seasonal forecasts show significant skill in predicting tropical precipitation anomalies up to six months ahead.
We evaluate ECMWF medium-range weather forecasts against observations to assess model performance over complex terrain.
The ECMWF Integrated Forecasting System is one of the most advanced numerical weather prediction models in the world.
ECMWF reanalysis products, including ERA5, are widely used as benchmark datasets for evaluating climate model performance.
We use ECMWF ensemble forecast data to assess uncertainty in projected precipitation over West Africa.
ECMWF subseasonal forecasts provide valuable information for anticipating extreme weather events two to four weeks ahead.
The ECMWF ocean model assimilates Argo float data to improve the initialization of coupled prediction systems.
ECMWF has developed a probabilistic framework for communicating forecast uncertainty to end users.
We compare ECMWF and NCEP ensemble forecasts to assess model differences in the prediction of blocking events.
ECMWF forecasts of Arctic sea ice extent provide critical information for shipping route planning in the Northern Sea Route.
CanESM5 exhibits a high equilibrium climate sensitivity driven by strong positive shortwave cloud feedbacks.
We use CanESM5 large ensemble simulations to assess the role of internal variability in observed precipitation trends.
CanESM5 projects a significant reduction in annual mean precipitation over the Mediterranean region under SSP5-8.5.
The interactive carbon cycle in CanESM5 allows for the simulation of climate-carbon cycle feedbacks under future scenarios.
CanESM5 simulates the observed historical warming trend with high fidelity across all major ocean basins.
We compare CanESM5 and GFDL-ESM4 projections of Arctic sea ice volume under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5.
CanESM5 projects an earlier timing of spring snowmelt over the Canadian prairies under all SSP scenarios.
The CanESM5 ensemble is used to evaluate the effectiveness of geoengineering strategies on regional climate.
CanESM5 historical simulations show a slight overestimation of observed warming rates in the tropical upper troposphere.
Land-use change emissions are explicitly represented in CanESM5, enabling assessment of their climate forcing.
GFDL-ESM4 projects a 35% reduction in global ocean primary productivity under SSP5-8.5 by 2100.
We evaluate GFDL-ESM4 performance in simulating the observed variability of the Pacific Decadal Oscillation.
GFDL-ESM4 includes interactive atmospheric chemistry, enabling assessment of climate-chemistry interactions.
The terrestrial carbon cycle in GFDL-ESM4 responds strongly to elevated CO2 through enhanced plant growth.
GFDL-ESM4 projects a significant intensification of tropical precipitation extremes under SSP3-7.0.
We use GFDL-ESM4 output to assess future changes in global fisheries productivity under warming scenarios.
GFDL-ESM4 simulates the observed spatial pattern of sea surface temperature trends with high fidelity.
The GFDL-ESM4 ocean model has a horizontal resolution of approximately 0.5° in both latitude and longitude.
GFDL-ESM4 projects a 20% decline in North Atlantic Deep Water formation by 2100 under SSP5-8.5.
Aerosol-cloud interactions in GFDL-ESM4 are parameterized using a two-moment microphysics scheme.
ACCESS-CM2 projects significant warming over the Australian continent under all SSP scenarios, with amplified inland warming.
We evaluate ACCESS-CM2 performance in simulating observed rainfall variability associated with the Indian Ocean Dipole.
ACCESS-CM2 includes an interactive ocean biogeochemistry module that simulates the marine carbon cycle.
The climate sensitivity of ACCESS-CM2 is estimated at 4.7°C, higher than the CMIP6 ensemble median.
ACCESS-CM2 projects increased frequency of marine heat waves around Australia under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5.
We use ACCESS-CM2 simulations to assess future changes in the Southern Annular Mode under greenhouse gas forcing.
ACCESS-CM2 simulates the observed interannual variability of Australian summer rainfall with reasonable skill.
The land surface scheme in ACCESS-CM2 has been updated to better represent Australian soil and vegetation types.
ACCESS-CM2 projects a significant reduction in winter rainfall over southern Australia by mid-century.
We compare ACCESS-CM2 and NorESM2 projections of Southern Ocean heat uptake under SSP5-8.5.
NorESM2 exhibits a lower climate sensitivity than most CMIP6 models, projecting moderate warming under high-emission scenarios.
We use NorESM2 to assess the impact of aerosol forcing on observed 20th century temperature trends.
NorESM2 includes an interactive ocean biogeochemistry component that represents the marine carbon and nitrogen cycles.
The representation of sea ice in NorESM2 has been substantially improved compared to its predecessor NorESM1.
NorESM2 projects a significant increase in the frequency of extreme precipitation events over Scandinavia.
We evaluate NorESM2 performance against ERA5 reanalysis for key atmospheric circulation indices over the North Atlantic.
NorESM2 simulates the observed decline in Arctic sea ice extent over the satellite era with high accuracy.
The ocean component of NorESM2 uses isopycnal coordinates to improve the representation of water mass formation.
NorESM2 projects a 25% reduction in winter precipitation over the Iberian Peninsula under SSP5-8.5.
Carbon cycle feedbacks in NorESM2 are assessed using the CMIP6 C4MIP protocol for idealized CO2 forcing experiments.
MIROC6 projects robust warming signals over the Asian continent under all SSP scenarios investigated.
We evaluate MIROC6 performance in simulating the observed interannual variability of the East Asian summer monsoon.
MIROC6 includes a detailed aerosol microphysics scheme that improves the simulation of aerosol-cloud interactions.
The climate sensitivity of MIROC6 is estimated at 2.6°C, among the lowest in the CMIP6 ensemble.
MIROC6 projects a significant increase in the frequency of compound heat and drought events over East Asia.
We use MIROC6 large ensemble simulations to assess internal variability in Japanese precipitation trends.
MIROC6 simulates the observed positive trend in global mean surface temperature since the pre-industrial era with good accuracy.
The ocean model in MIROC6 resolves mesoscale eddies, improving the simulation of ocean heat transport.
MIROC6 projects a poleward expansion of the subtropical dry zone under SSP5-8.5 forcing.
Vegetation-climate feedbacks in MIROC6 are assessed using a coupled land surface and atmospheric model configuration.
BCC-CSM2 projects significant warming over the Tibetan Plateau, with implications for regional water resources.
We evaluate BCC-CSM2 performance in simulating the observed variability of the East Asian winter monsoon.
BCC-CSM2 includes an updated atmospheric dynamical core that improves the simulation of planetary wave propagation.
The climate sensitivity of BCC-CSM2 is estimated at 3.0°C, close to the CMIP6 ensemble median.
BCC-CSM2 projects increased frequency of extreme precipitation events over the Yangtze River basin under SSP5-8.5.
We use BCC-CSM2 simulations to assess future changes in the South Asian summer monsoon under SSP2-4.5.
BCC-CSM2 historical simulations show a slight warm bias over the North Pacific during boreal winter.
The land surface model in BCC-CSM2 includes a dynamic global vegetation component for long-term simulations.
BCC-CSM2 projects a significant reduction in permafrost extent over the Siberian lowlands under all SSP scenarios.
Ocean heat uptake in BCC-CSM2 is evaluated against Argo float observations and satellite altimetry data.
INM-CM5 exhibits one of the lowest equilibrium climate sensitivities among CMIP6 models at approximately 1.8°C.
We evaluate INM-CM5 performance in simulating observed Northern Hemisphere sea level pressure patterns.
INM-CM5 projects moderate warming under all SSP scenarios, consistent with its low climate sensitivity.
The ocean component of INM-CM5 has a higher horizontal resolution than its predecessor, improving circulation dynamics.
INM-CM5 simulates the observed historical warming trend, although with a slower pace than the multi-model mean.
We use INM-CM5 output to assess future changes in European blocking frequency under SSP2-4.5.
INM-CM5 projects a smaller reduction in Arctic sea ice extent than most other CMIP6 models under high-emission scenarios.
The atmospheric dynamics in INM-CM5 are evaluated against ERA5 reanalysis for the period 1979 to 2014.
INM-CM5 shows improved representation of the observed El Niño-Southern Oscillation amplitude compared to INM-CM4.
Carbon cycle feedbacks in INM-CM5 are weaker than in most CMIP6 models due to differences in land surface parameterizations.
